# A reader's guide to Bayesian results {#sec-bridge}

::: {.callout-note}
Drafted by a LLM-based AI tool (Claude Code/Opus 4.8).
:::

This chapter is a self-contained guide to reading the results in Parts IV–VI. It
assumes you are comfortable with p-values, confidence intervals and effect sizes, and
newer to Bayesian methods; each Bayesian idea is introduced in those terms first.
Nothing here is novel statistics — it is the standard effect-existence-versus-
significance argument [@makowski2019], a retrospective design analysis
[@gelman2014design], and a region of practical equivalence [@kruschke2018rope], applied
to this study and turned into a house rule.

## Why Bayesian, and how to read a posterior {#sec-bridge-why}

A frequentist analysis returns a point estimate and a standard error. A Bayesian model
returns a whole *distribution* for the unknown effect — the **posterior** — given the
data and the model. Every number we report is a summary of that distribution.

- The **posterior median** is the central estimate (the analogue of a point estimate).
  We use the median rather than the mean because it is *transformation-invariant*: the
  median on the log-odds scale maps to the median on the items scale, whereas the mean
  does not. This matters because we report each effect on two scales (below).
- The **posterior standard deviation** plays the role of the standard error.
- The **95% credible interval (CrI)** is the central range holding 95% of the posterior.
  We report **equal-tailed** intervals (the 2.5th to 97.5th percentile) throughout; the
  only exception is the per-parameter convergence table in @sec-computation, which
  displays a highest-density interval.

Two scales appear together for every treatment effect. The **logit (log-odds) scale**
is the model's natural parameter, written $\tau$. The **items scale** is the practically
meaningful one: the average number of extra test items a child gets correct because of
the intervention (computed per posterior draw by toggling each child's randomised
assignment and averaging the change in expected items-correct, so it carries the full
uncertainty). Throughout, **positive $\tau$ means the intervention raised the outcome**,
and **only $\tau$ is causal** — it is identified by randomisation (@sec-dag); every other
coefficient in the report is an adjusted association.

## Five translations for the frequentist reader {#sec-bridge-translations}

1. **A credible interval is not a confidence interval.** A 95% CrI genuinely licenses
   the sentence everyone *wishes* they could say of a confidence interval: "given the
   data and the model, there is a 95% probability the effect lies in this range." A
   confidence interval is a property of the *procedure* over hypothetical repeated
   samples and does not license that statement about this one interval.
2. **A posterior tail probability is not a p-value.** We quote
   $\text{pd} = P(\tau > 0)$, the share of the posterior above zero — informally, "the
   probability the intervention helped." A p-value is $P(\text{data this extreme}\mid
   \text{null is true})$ — a different conditional, and not the probability that any
   hypothesis is true.
3. **A ROPE decision is not a significance test.** Practical conclusions come from where
   the posterior sits relative to a pre-stated *region of practical equivalence* around
   zero — an explicit, magnitude-based judgement — not from crossing $p = 0.05$.
4. **There is no "statistical significance."** We report estimates with their
   uncertainty and a magnitude decision; we deliberately avoid the binary
   significant/non-significant vocabulary and the ritual it implies [@amrhein2019].
5. **Evidence can support a null, not only fail to reject it.** A frequentist test can
   only fail to reject; a Bayesian analysis can report *positive* evidence that an effect
   is practically negligible — the posterior mass inside the ROPE. This matters here for
   honestly reporting the near-zero and floored effects.

## The probability-of-direction trap {#sec-bridge-pd}

It is tempting to treat $\text{pd}$ as a clean Bayesian replacement for the p-value. It
is not — it is almost a *re-expression* of it. For approximately symmetric posteriors
under weak priors (our case), $p \approx 2\,(1 - \text{pd})$ [@makowski2019]: so
$\text{pd} = 0.975 \approx p = 0.05$, and $\text{pd} = 0.90 \approx p = 0.20$. A threshold
on $\text{pd}$ is therefore a p-value threshold wearing Bayesian clothes, and — like the
p-value — it answers only **existence and sign**, never **magnitude**. On a small sample
you can have $\text{pd} = 0.99$ for an effect too small to matter, or $\text{pd} = 0.85$
for an important one. This is why the report never stops at $\text{pd}$: every effect is
reported for **direction and magnitude separately**.

## Direction, magnitude, and the evidence ladder {#sec-bridge-evidence}

**Magnitude** is judged against a **minimally-important difference** $\delta$ — the
smallest effect of interest, set on the items scale per outcome. (Frequentists meet the
same idea as the smallest effect size of interest in equivalence testing
[@lakens2018], or a clinical minimally-important difference.) A band $[-\delta, +\delta]$
around zero is the **region of practical equivalence (ROPE)**: an effect inside it is
"practically the same as none." The $\delta$ values are a domain judgement, **provisional**
and owned by the education lead (@sec-priors).

Each effect is then reported as a small card, not a single number:

- the effect on the **items scale**, posterior **median** with a **50% and a 95%**
  credible interval (two intervals, so no single boundary carries the decision);
- **direction** — $\text{pd} = P(\tau > 0)$;
- **meaningful benefit** — $P(\text{benefit} \ge \delta)$, plus the ROPE coverage
  $P(|\text{effect}| < \delta)$;
- a **magnitude-reliability flag** where the effect is imprecisely estimated (next
  section).

Where prose forces a verbal label, we use one odds-based ladder, applied to whichever
probability the claim is about, and we always append the word *evidence* (the label
qualifies the *evidence*, never the effect's *size*) and show the odds beside it. The
round-number odds boundaries are deliberately **not** the $p = 0.05/0.025/0.01$ images,
so they do not smuggle the significance grid back in:

| Posterior probability | Approx. odds | Evidence label |
|---|---|---|
| 0.50 – 0.75 | up to 3 : 1 | inconclusive |
| 0.75 – 0.91 | 3 : 1 – 10 : 1 | suggestive |
| 0.91 – 0.97 | 10 : 1 – 30 : 1 | moderate |
| 0.97 – 0.99 | 30 : 1 – 100 : 1 | strong |
| > 0.99 | > 100 : 1 | very strong |

: The evidence ladder. Run separately for *direction* and *meaningful benefit*, which routinely differ. {#tbl-evidence-ladder}

## Small-sample honesty: Type-S and Type-M {#sec-bridge-small-sample}

With about 54 children, the binding risk is not only that we miss real effects (the
classical worry) but that **even our apparent hits are unreliable in sign and inflated
in size**. When attention is restricted to estimates that clear a bar — the 95% interval
excludes zero — we are selecting on a noisy quantity, and selection on noise has a
systematic consequence: among the survivors, the magnitude is on average too *large*,
and, where power is low, the *sign* can be wrong. This is the "winner's curse," measured
by a retrospective **design analysis** [@gelman2014design]: the **Type-S** (sign) error
rate and the **Type-M** (magnitude exaggeration) ratio.

The key number: a genuinely real effect sitting exactly on the conventional "strong" cut
(estimate $= 1.96$ standard errors from zero, i.e. the 95% interval just excludes zero)
is still, on average, exaggerated **≈ 1.4×** when it clears the bar. Clearing the bar
certifies **direction**, not **size**. So we **lead with the interval, not the point
estimate**, and flag effects whose precision is low.

The distinction this protects is best seen in two of the study's own outcomes (a preview
of @sec-itt; figures are preliminary dev-configuration fits). Under the old "is the 95%
interval clear of zero?" habit, letter–sound knowledge and word reading were
*indistinguishable* — both "strong." The direction-and-magnitude view separates them:

| | Letter sounds (32-item test) | Word reading (79-item test) |
|---|---|---|
| Effect (items, median; 95% CrI) | +3.6 items [+1.3, +5.7] | +2.4 items [+0.3, +4.4] |
| Direction ($\text{pd}$) | 0.999 (very strong evidence it helps) | 0.989 (strong evidence it helps) |
| Meaningful benefit | $P(\ge 2\text{ items}) = 0.91$ | $P(\ge 2\text{ items}) = 0.64$ |
| In-ROPE ($\pm$ 2 items) | 0.09 | 0.36 |
| Magnitude reliability (Type-M) | ≈ 1.07 (well powered) | ≈ 1.28 (point estimate optimistic) |

In plain words: letter sounds is a *meaningful* effect that is also well-powered, so its
size can be trusted. Word reading is *near-certain in direction but uncertain in size* —
a benefit is almost surely present, but there is a real (≈ 36%) chance it is smaller than
two items, and the point estimate is itself mildly inflated. "Meaningful" versus "real
but maybe small" is exactly the distinction a single $\text{pd}$ threshold discards — and
the reason the results chapters report the whole card.
